quiz_questions.question_text: What is a scalar quantity?

options.text: A quantity measured with a ruler
A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A quantity that can only be positive
A quantity that has only magnitude (size) and no direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Scalar quantities are fully described by a magnitude (numerical value with a unit) alone. Examples include mass, temperature, speed, energy, and time.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a vector quantity?

options.text: A quantity that is always positive
A quantity that has only magnitude
A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A quantity that cannot be measured

quiz_questions.explanation: Vector quantities require both a magnitude and a direction to be fully described. Examples include displacement, velocity, force, and acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?

options.text: Force
Velocity
Displacement
Temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Temperature has magnitude only (e.g., 25°C) and no associated direction. Velocity, displacement, and force are all vector quantities.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a vector quantity?

options.text: Mass
Speed
Displacement
Energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement has both magnitude (e.g., 5 m) and direction (e.g., north). Mass, speed, and energy are scalar quantities.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between speed and velocity?

options.text: Velocity applies only to cars, speed to all objects
Speed is faster than velocity
There is no difference; they are the same thing
Speed is a scalar (magnitude only), while velocity is a vector (magnitude and direction)

quiz_questions.explanation: Speed tells you how fast something moves (e.g., 60 km/h) but not where it's going. Velocity includes direction (e.g., 60 km/h north), making it a vector.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between distance and displacement?

options.text: Displacement measures curved paths while distance measures straight lines
Distance is a scalar (total path length), while displacement is a vector (straight-line distance from start to finish with direction)
They are different words for the same measurement
Distance is always larger than displacement

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance measures the total path traveled regardless of direction. Displacement measures the straight-line distance and direction from the starting point to the ending point.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two forces of 3 N and 4 N act on an object at right angles. What is the magnitude of the resultant force?

options.text: 1 N
5 N
7 N

quiz_questions.explanation: Using the Pythagorean theorem for perpendicular vectors: √(3² + 4²) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a scalar?

options.text: Time
Weight
Momentum
Acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: Time has magnitude only (e.g., 5 seconds) and no direction. Acceleration, momentum, and weight are vectors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person walks 3 km north then 4 km south. What is the magnitude of their displacement?

options.text: 1 km north
7 km
1 km south
7 km south

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish: 3 km north − 4 km south = 1 km south (net movement). The total distance traveled would be 7 km.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you add two vectors that point in the same direction?

options.text: Subtract the smaller from the larger
Use the Pythagorean theorem
Add their magnitudes — the resultant has the same direction
Multiply their magnitudes together

quiz_questions.explanation: When vectors point in the same direction, their magnitudes simply add. For example, two forces of 5 N and 3 N both pushing right produce a resultant of 8 N to the right.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a scalar quantity?

options.text: A physical quantity that requires a direction for its description
A quantity that is always positive
A physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude alone and has no associated direction
A quantity that can only be measured with digital instruments

quiz_questions.explanation: Scalars are quantities like mass, temperature, speed, and energy that are fully specified by a number and unit. No direction information is needed or meaningful.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a vector quantity?

options.text: A quantity that has only size and no other properties
A physical quantity that requires both magnitude and direction for a complete description
A quantity measured along a straight line
A quantity expressed using arrows in textbooks

quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors like displacement, velocity, force, and acceleration are not fully described without specifying a direction. They are represented graphically as arrows where length indicates magnitude and the arrow indicates direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the resultant vector?

options.text: A vector that cancels out all other vectors
The first vector in a series of additions
The largest of all contributing vectors
The single vector that produces the same effect as two or more individual vectors combined

quiz_questions.explanation: The resultant is the net vector obtained by vector addition. It can be found graphically (tip-to-tail method) or analytically (component method). The resultant produces the same physical effect as all the original vectors acting together.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes vector addition?

options.text: Simply adding the numerical values of any two quantities
Arranging vectors in alphabetical order
The process of combining two or more vectors according to both their magnitudes and directions to find a single resultant vector
Multiplying vectors together to get a larger quantity

quiz_questions.explanation: Vector addition accounts for direction, not just magnitude. Two vectors of equal magnitude can produce a resultant anywhere from zero (if opposite) to double (if same direction), depending on the angle between them.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes displacement?

options.text: The total path length traveled by an object
The speed of an object multiplied by time
The distance an object covers in one second
A vector quantity equal to the straight-line distance and direction from an initial position to a final position

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is a vector: it has a magnitude (the shortest distance between two points) and a direction. A round trip has zero displacement but nonzero distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes velocity?

options.text: The acceleration of an object multiplied by time
How fast an object moves, regardless of direction
The distance traveled per unit time
A vector quantity that describes the rate of change of displacement with respect to time, including direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity is the vector counterpart of speed. It specifies both how fast an object moves and the direction of motion. Average velocity = displacement / time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the component method of vector addition?

options.text: A technique that adds vector magnitudes regardless of direction
A method that only works for vectors in the same direction
A technique that resolves each vector into perpendicular components (typically x and y), adds components separately, then recombines to find the resultant
A graphical method using protractors and rulers

quiz_questions.explanation: The component method breaks each vector into x and y parts using trigonometry: Vx = V cos θ, Vy = V sin θ. After adding all x-components and all y-components separately, the resultant magnitude is √(Rx² + Ry²) and direction is θ = tan⁻¹(Ry/Rx).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the tip-to-tail method?

options.text: A method of subtracting vectors by reversing their direction
A way of measuring the angle between vectors
A graphical vector addition technique where each successive vector is drawn starting from the tip (head) of the previous vector
A technique that only works for two vectors

quiz_questions.explanation: In the tip-to-tail method, vectors are drawn head-to-tail in sequence. The resultant is the vector drawn from the tail of the first vector to the tip of the last vector. This method works for any number of vectors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a unit vector?

options.text: A vector that points upward
The smallest possible vector
A vector expressed in SI base units
A vector with a magnitude of exactly one, used to indicate direction only

quiz_questions.explanation: Unit vectors have magnitude 1 and specify direction only. Common unit vectors are î (x-direction), ĵ (y-direction), and k̂ (z-direction). Any vector can be expressed as a magnitude multiplied by its unit vector.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes equilibrium in terms of vectors?

options.text: A state where the vector sum (resultant) of all forces acting on an object equals zero
A condition where no forces act on an object
A situation where only one force acts on an object
A state where all forces have the same magnitude

quiz_questions.explanation: An object is in equilibrium when the net force (vector sum of all forces) is zero. This can occur with many forces acting, as long as they all cancel out. The object may be stationary or moving at constant velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pilot flies 200 km east, then 150 km north. She needs to calculate the displacement from her starting airport to plan the return flight. What is the magnitude and direction of her displacement?

options.text: 250 km at 36.9° north of east — using the Pythagorean theorem √(200² + 150²) = 250 km, and tan θ = 150/200 gives θ = 36.9°
50 km northeast, particularly regarding return under the given assumptions
350 km in the direction of her last heading as typically measured in practice
200 km east since that was the longer leg when considering all relevant factors

quiz_questions.explanation: The two legs are perpendicular, forming a right triangle. Resultant magnitude: √(200² + 150²) = √(40,000 + 22,500) = √62,500 = 250 km. Direction: θ = tan⁻¹(150/200) = 36.9° north of east.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hiker walks 5 km north, then 5 km east, then 5 km south. What is his displacement from where he started?

options.text: 5 km east — the northward and southward components cancel, leaving only the eastward displacement
15 km in total, particularly regarding walks when applying fundamental principles
0 km — he returned to where he started when evaluated systematically
10 km northeast, particularly regarding started when measured under controlled conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: Breaking into components: North-south = 5 km N − 5 km S = 0. East-west = 5 km E. The northward and southward legs cancel, leaving a net displacement of 5 km east.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students argue about whether kinetic energy is a vector or scalar. Student A says it's a vector because a moving object has a direction. Student B says it's scalar because energy has no direction. Who is correct and why?

options.text: Both are correct depending on the reference frame
Student B — kinetic energy (½mv²) is a scalar; it depends on the square of speed (a scalar) and has no directional property
Neither — kinetic energy is neither scalar nor vector
Student A — anything involving motion must be a vector

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv² is always positive and has no direction. While the object moves in a direction, the energy itself does not — it's the same regardless of which direction the object moves. Squaring velocity (v²) removes all directional information.

quiz_questions.question_text: A boat aims due north across a river at 4 m/s while the river current flows east at 3 m/s. What is the boat's actual velocity relative to the ground?

options.text: 7 m/s due northeast, particularly regarding direction as observed in standard experiments
1 m/s due north, particularly regarding course as observed in standard experiments
5 m/s at 36.9° east of north — the perpendicular velocities combine via the Pythagorean theorem: √(4² + 3²) = 5 m/s
4 m/s due north because the boat's engine determines its direction

quiz_questions.explanation: The boat's velocity and the river current are perpendicular vectors. The resultant speed is √(4² + 3²) = √25 = 5 m/s. The direction is θ = tan⁻¹(3/4) = 36.9° east of north, showing the current pushes the boat off course.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car drives around a circular track at a constant speed of 60 km/h. A physics student says the velocity is changing even though the speed is constant. Is the student correct?

options.text: Yes — but only because the car's mass changes during the lap
No — velocity only changes when the car speeds up or slows down
No — if speed is constant, velocity is also constant
Yes — velocity is a vector, so even though the magnitude (speed) is constant, the direction is continuously changing as the car goes around the track

quiz_questions.explanation: Since velocity is a vector, it has both magnitude (speed) and direction. On a circular track, the direction of motion constantly changes even at constant speed, meaning the velocity vector is always changing. This changing velocity means the car is accelerating (centripetal acceleration).

quiz_questions.question_text: An airplane flying at 250 km/h encounters a headwind of 50 km/h. The pilot reports a ground speed of 200 km/h. This situation illustrates vector addition of velocities because:

options.text: 200 km/h is the arithmetic mean of 250 and 50
The airplane must burn more fuel in headwinds
Headwinds always cut airplane speed in half
The airplane velocity and wind velocity are opposite vectors that partially cancel, reducing the resultant ground speed

quiz_questions.explanation: When vectors are in opposite directions, the resultant magnitude is the difference: 250 − 50 = 200 km/h. This is a straightforward case of vector addition where the velocity vectors are antiparallel.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physics teacher pushes a box with a force of 100 N at a 30° angle below the horizontal. A student asks: 'How much of this force is actually moving the box horizontally?' What is the horizontal component?

options.text: 86.6 N — the horizontal component is F cos 30° = 100 × 0.866 = 86.6 N
50 N — the horizontal component is always half the total force
100 N — the full force moves the box horizontally
30 N — the angle directly gives the component

quiz_questions.explanation: The horizontal component of a vector at angle θ from horizontal is Fx = F cos θ. Here, Fx = 100 cos 30° = 100 × 0.866 = 86.6 N. The vertical component (100 sin 30° = 50 N) pushes the box into the floor.

quiz_questions.question_text: A soccer player kicks a ball at 20 m/s at 45° above the horizontal. The initial horizontal and vertical velocity components are each about 14.1 m/s. During flight, which component changes and which stays constant (ignoring air resistance)?

options.text: The vertical component changes due to gravity while the horizontal component remains constant — gravity only acts vertically
Both components change equally due to gravity
The horizontal component decreases while the vertical stays constant
Both components remain constant during flight

quiz_questions.explanation: In projectile motion (ignoring air resistance), the horizontal and vertical components are independent. Gravity acts only vertically (changing vy), while no horizontal force acts on the ball, keeping vx constant at 14.1 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Three tug-of-war teams pull on a ring. Team A pulls 500 N north, Team B pulls 500 N east, and Team C pulls 707 N southwest (exactly 45° south of west). Is the ring in equilibrium?

options.text: Yes — Team C's force exactly cancels Teams A and B's resultant, making the net force zero
No — three forces can never produce equilibrium
No — Team C's force is larger than either A or B
Yes — but only because all three forces are equal

quiz_questions.explanation: Teams A and B produce a resultant of √(500² + 500²) = 707 N directed northeast. Team C pulls 707 N in exactly the opposite direction (southwest). The vector sum is zero, so the ring is in equilibrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: A smartphone compass app shows a hiker's displacement as 1.2 km at bearing 060° (60° east of north). The hiker's friend asks for the displacement in terms of 'how far east and how far north.' What are the rectangular components?

options.text: 1.2 km east and 1.2 km north
1.04 km east and 0.60 km north — east = 1.2 sin 60° = 1.04, north = 1.2 cos 60° = 0.60
0.60 km east and 1.04 km north
0.85 km east and 0.85 km north

quiz_questions.explanation: For a bearing of 060° (measured clockwise from north): East component = 1.2 sin 60° = 1.2 × 0.866 = 1.04 km. North component = 1.2 cos 60° = 1.2 × 0.500 = 0.60 km. Note that bearing angles use north as reference, not east.
